item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is so good. very rarely does an article make you FEEL GOOD after reading it. These vibes emanate from all that you do. Keep doing it. +1

item.title: Re: Unifying the links in the article
item.content:encoded: And of course, in my hastened typing I fuck up the spacing IN THE TITLE. I suck, sorry.

item.title: Unifying the links int he article
item.content:encoded:
At least one link in the article each leads to a different version of the website. One goes to the dot net, one to the dot com, and one to the wikidot dot com.

Can we somehow unify these to all go to the wikidot version? For some reason my personal account only shows up on the wikidot one, and that's a problem that's apparently existed for people for years. The dot com would be fine too, but for some reason the dot net link now does a redirect to some unrelated site, possibly a phishing site.

If someone could look at this and at the very least change the dot net one, they would be awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Maravilla: (takes a sip of water)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just noting that I've reverted the latest edit. "Anartist" is actually a term used on the wiki, not a spacing error. It means an artist who uses anomalies in their art. For more information about anartists, have a look at the Are We Cool Yet? Hub.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Good ideas; I've added them to the proposal.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
(God I keep forgetting to respond to these)

I'm kinda iffy on linking just a hub unless that's the only option.

For starters, D-19060 returns in SCP-3132. I've previously crosslinked D-11424 to one of his other appearances in here already.

There is a tale featuring the entities in this skip, Joey Fucknuts Takes To The Skies, but I can't seem to find a place in the conversation where the link "fits"; open to suggestions.

Additionally, perhaps an opinion/politics/art-interpretation-related SCP could be crosslinked or mentioned in the procedures.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP mentions Are We Cool Yet?, which has a hub. I propose linking to the hub.

Editing this:

PoI-0171 was apprehended during Incident-████-9F92, involving AWCY?

To this:

PoI-0171 was apprehended during Incident-████-9F92, involving AWCY?

Editing this:

D-19060, a 22-year-old African-American man.

To this:

D-19060, a 22-year-old African-American man.

Editing this:

SCP-3435 is an oil painting by known anartist Guillermo Gutierrez, titled "Dream a Better Dream", measuring 110 cm by 68 cm.

To this:

SCP-3435 is an oil painting by known anartist Guillermo Gutierrez, titled "Dream a Better Dream", measuring 110 cm by 68 cm.

Adding this to the end of the Special Containment Procedures:

Under no circumstances are any personnel to be assigned to both SCP-3435 and SCP-2078 at the same time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might be talking about The Cool War tales?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really loved a lot of this; it felt sentimental in all the right ways. I would disagree with the “who cares, there’s no big message” idea (art is always reflective of the beliefs of its creator on some level, and any interpretation that can be argued to make sense is valid) but that’s more a personal thing. The interview really does drag on a lot and while it’s kind of anvilicious there are some beautiful lines in there and the rest of the article is joyful enough to make up for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an art™, I love this skip so much. There's more than a few people who insist that all art has to be meaningful, entwined in the contemporary culture and presenting insightful commentary on contemporary issues (obviously the commentary has to agree with what those people believe), and god forbid it be pleasant or fun or just plain nice. I don't want to make dark, grim art that condemns social media and uplifts the status quo of 20-odd years ago. If I just want to make someone grin like a kid and go "wow, cool," that's just as valid!

+1, and wow, cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really profoundly disagree with this guy's stance pretty much entirely, and while I get that the piece is supposed to be ambiguous about the validity of his position, it still ends up feeling like it's pandering to that crowd to a certain extent with the chummy nature of the interview and the effect being unambiguously positive so long as you're not one of those stick-in-the-muds who enjoys thinking deeply about art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how little sense this makes and how specific the effect is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, I'm glad you liked it!

I'd definitely love to write more stuff with Guillermo, I love his character; it's just really difficult to channel his POV without good inspiration or ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally? I love that. We need more of that in the world and less grimdark BS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love you. You beautiful bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me that interview was more about opening up that relationship between Gutierrez and Maravilla than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A skip that isn't just "non-spooky," but inspirational.

An anartist that genuinely sees the benefit of something JUST being cool. Who's cooler than cool.

I would upvote this fifty times if I could. I'd love to see more of Guillermo in the future. A hypercompetent dude who uses his many talents to make the world a bit better, and cooler.

Every time I drift away from the wiki, I eventually find something that makes me want to kick myself for drifting. Last time it was the entire AWCY story chain involving The Sculpture, Nobody, and a bunch of others (can't remember the story name). This time it's this SCP. Awesome freaking job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the last interview sorta overexplains everything? Like before that, I was thinking "oh I get it it's a piece of art which deliberately avoids interpretation as a metaphor" and it sorta bugs me that you splay it out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OK so picture something like this:

+ show block
– hide block

But with slightly more articulated fingers where those two mid-wing claws at the left edge of the picture are. That's what he holds his staff and spellbook with. The wyvern stands most of the time, and walks on the two feet with the aid of the staff, but holds the stuff in his feet when flying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One kinda weird question, because I'm having trouble picturing the painting: how is the wyvern holding the staff and book? Wyverns are built like birds, with one pair of legs for standing on and one pair of wings, so is it flying with the stuff held in its feet…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview dragged on a bit and comes across as telling, not showing, but it's still pretty damn neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what? I just might now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Remember what's really important here."

That was the line that got my vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I highly suggest you commission someone to create the actual painting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a wyvern dressed up as a wizard. A wizvern, if you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what the painting looks like. Is it a wizard or a wyvern?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3435 depicts two entities: a blue theropod dinosaur with cybernetic attachments and various laser weapons reminiscent of those in science fiction; and a red European-style wyvern dressed in a pointed hat and colorful robe, holding a staff and what appears to be a spellbook.

I know some look at the over-interpretation of art, but a cool-looking imagery is good enough and saves me the trouble of thinking too much about things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The most important things first: Curse you for making me upvote an AWCY? scip.

As for the scip itself, I really like it. I think the interview overdid it a bit on the exposition front but it was really enjoyable to read nevertheless. The interviews with the D-Class felt a bit clunky in comparison but ultimately I have to upvote because, seriously, fuck the over-interpretation of art. Sometimes the cigar is really just a fucking cigar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that someone new will probably get to SCP-1057 or the like before this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's really unfortunate, because once upon a time, AWCY wasn't the butt of every joke.

You say that like its a bad thing. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's really unfortunate, because once upon a time, AWCY wasn't the butt of every joke. :/ I have to wonder how they look to someone who's reading through the wiki for the first time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I know that was your intent, I'll cancel my downvote. Now I'm laughing at myself for only reaching that conclusion about this article by doing what Gutierrez made fun of. Maybe if this was hinted at a bit more in the article, it might lead on some others.

Also, there's something really funny about you over-analyzing a work of art about over-analyzing works of art

I totally recognized I was doing this but I couldn't stop myself :')

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't this a contradiction?

Yes.

For all of Gutierrez's optimism and freshness, he's just as fallible and hypocritical as he accuses AWCY of being. Note that he doesn't say his way is the only way, just that it should be a perfectly valid option.

But he's not saying that art shouldn't have deeper meaning at all, he's saying that it should be just as acceptable if it doesn't, and it's specifically negative, pessimistic messages in art that he rails against. The last D-class didn't interpret 3435 quite the same way as Gutierrez intended, but he was still free of the side effects and got the clearest dreams of anyone tested.

Also, there's something really funny about you over-analyzing a work of art about over-analyzing works of art :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gutierrez is just a guy; no reason he can't be a little hypocritical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The title of the painting is a reference to something else entirely :P Most of the dreams are references and mixes of dreams I've actually had.

I took a page from Djoric, when he said in a discussion page that in-universe, AWCY was a decaying shell of its former self, and if an anartist was any good, they probably weren't part of it.

The thing I was trying to get across was that Palanez was more interested in the opportunity to catch up with his old friend, under the guise of assisting the investigation somehow. Originally I had Gutierrez escape during the interview through anomalous means by way of Palanez letting his guard down, but couldn't figure out how to make that work.

The note's supposed to reflect that the Director's not falling for it, as well as a sort of take-that to articles with the "Staff may request to use SCP-XXX for…" and "Obsessive researcher keeps studying something even when there's nothing to be learned" tropes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You gotta dream a better dream. Upvote because the interview is so good.

What can I say? This doesn't quite hit in my usual aversion to dream skips, possibly because the dream effects aren't central to the whole thing. There's other stuff going on here, is what I mean. The direct attempt to prevent reading meaning into it, coupled with the interview, actually brings this back to the soul of AWCY, I think. I mean, I can definitely see them getting mad about something like this.

My only real qualm is the final note, which is kind of dramatic and almost lolFoundationy, and just doesn't add anything. I mean, if you want to explain why the Doctor would be allowed to be so chummy with this guy, there could be a note at the end of the interview about him being reprimanded for unprofessional behavior or something. That's about the only purpose I see it serving, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no art expert. But after some thought, I realized what my beef with this was.

Gutierrez says that AWCY? is silly for constantly forcing messages with its art, and that they take themselves too seriously— that we should revel in art's ability to be sandboxes for ideas and creativity, as an escape from real world ideas.

Meanwhile, the anomaly is blatantly pushing a message itself: that art doesn't need to have any deeper value besides a surface-level emotion, like "cool". It punishes the viewer for trying to derive insight from it, even when that's where the pleasure lies in observing art. All because Gutierrez wants to tell anyone seeing it, "you're too much of an adult, I just made this because dinos." That in itself is art with a message.

Isn't this a contradiction?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did upvote, but I see what you mean. It was pretty obvious what the purpose of the painting was, so having it spelled out in the interview seemed unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incidentally, "non-clinical things being described in a clinical way" is one of my favorite elements of this site. I'm glad you liked it :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to disagree with you on this.

That was some of the most natural dialogue in an interview I've seen, if you strip out the pleasantries and shorthands from actual conversation.

It felt like two people actually talking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially, I loved this, and was this close to upvoting. Then I go to the interview log, and that completely killed it for me. Not only did it turn the already-extant implications of the article into clunky exposition, it was cheesy and didn't seem very realistic. Any new information it provided seemed entirely unnecessary.

If it hadn't been for the interview, I would have upvoted this like a shot. As it is, I have to downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a fraught relationship with art and its creation. This hit me where my feels ought to be. The interview with the non-anart anomalous artist really sold it for me. He knows what he wants his art to be, and that's what he does. AWCY? can go perne in a gyre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you're right, dialed it back a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not the sole reason for my vote, but

I see art as an escape. A way to look at better worlds, at brighter days. With paint, clay, words, instruments? The sky's the limit! You can make new things, impossible things, other worlds! Art lets you make things you wouldn't see otherwise!

Unbearably cheesy, it sounds like it came out of a children's show. I hope nobody ever says something like this to my face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is my spirit animal. It reminds me of how much I hated trying to find meaning in Chaucer back in college. I mean, I was probably an idiot back then, but that doesn't take away anything from how much I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For one, this is an awesome skip, and I'm glad to see an AWCY? skip that really performs well. This feels like a more "tight" than the usual AWCY? article, and an excellent example of nailing tone to the wall.

For two:

SCP-3435 depicts two entities: a blue theropod dinosaur with cybernetic attachments and various laser weapons reminiscent of those in science fiction; and a red European-style wyvern dressed in a pointed hat and colorful robe, holding a staff and what appears to be a spellbook.

This is the greatest thing I've ever read in my life. A kick-ass example of non-clinical things being described in a clinical way. +1

item.title: Everything that is, or was, began with a dream.
item.content:encoded:
Alrighty, here we go. Trying to branch out with something different here.

Big thanks to Dr_Leonard, DrBleep, Leveritas, 9Volt, and trennerdios for looking at this.

Is this commentary on something? Meta-commentary? You decide.
